claims.evidence_text: Wheat seeds can germinate across a wide temperature range – from as low as 4°C to as high as 37°C.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat germinates over a range of 4 to 37 C.

claims.evidence_text: However, the optimal germination temperature falls between 12°C and 25°C . Temperatures of 20-25°C produce the fastest germination, while slightly cooler temperatures of 12-17°C tend to produce the most seedlings.
claims.claim_summary: Optimal wheat germination temperature is 12 to 25 C.

claims.evidence_text: During the vegetative phase – which includes tillering, leaf production, and root development – wheat performs best at temperatures between 16°C and 25°C .
claims.claim_summary: Optimum vegetative-phase temperature for wheat is 16 to 25 C.

claims.evidence_text: According to the FAO’s crop information database , the mean daily temperature for optimum growth and tillering is between 15°C and 20°C.
claims.claim_summary: FAO: mean daily temperature for optimum growth and tillering is 15 to 20 C.

claims.evidence_text: As wheat transitions from vegetative growth to flowering and grain filling, it actually prefers cooler temperatures – around 14°C . Cooler conditions during this stage allow better starch accumulation in the grains
claims.claim_summary: Wheat prefers cooler temperatures around 14 C during flowering and grain filling.

claims.evidence_text: Research from NC State Extension shows that temperatures between approximately 7°C and 24°C (45°F to 75°F) during the jointing-to-bloom period support optimal growth and kernel development.
claims.claim_summary: Temperatures of 7 to 24 C during jointing-to-bloom support optimal growth and kernel development.

claims.evidence_text: Temperatures above 30°C during grain filling can reduce starch deposition and grain weight significantly. Prolonged exposure to canopy temperatures above 30°C causes early plant senescence and ends grain growth prematurely.
claims.claim_summary: Temperatures above 30 C during grain filling reduce starch and grain weight and cause early senescence.

claims.evidence_text: Temperatures above 30°C during grain filling can reduce starch deposition and grain weight significantly. Prolonged exposure to canopy temperatures above 30°C causes early plant senescence and ends grain growth prematurely.
claims.claim_summary: Grain filling is heat-sensitive; canopy temperatures above 30 C end grain growth prematurely.

claims.evidence_text: Winter wheat requires a period of cold exposure (known as vernalization ) at temperatures between 0°C and 5°C for normal heading and flowering.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat requires vernalization at 0 to 5 C for normal heading and flowering.

claims.evidence_text: In their early stages, winter wheat varieties can tolerate extreme cold down to -20°C, but this frost resistance is gradually lost as the plant approaches heading.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat tolerates cold down to -20 C in early stages, losing frost resistance toward heading.

claims.evidence_text: Spring wheat does not need vernalization and can be planted in spring.
claims.claim_summary: Spring wheat does not require vernalization.

claims.evidence_text: According to FAO data , about three-fourths of the world’s wheat-growing areas receive between 375 mm and 875 mm (roughly 37.5 cm to 87.5 cm) of annual precipitation.
claims.claim_summary: Three-fourths of wheat-growing areas receive 375-875 mm annual precipitation.

claims.evidence_text: However, the crop can technically be grown in areas receiving anywhere from 250 mm to 1,750 mm of annual rainfall.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat can be grown in areas receiving 250-1750 mm annual rainfall.

claims.evidence_text: The commonly cited optimal range for wheat is 25 cm to 150 cm of annual rainfall . Within this range, the crop receives enough moisture without the risk of waterlogging.
claims.claim_summary: Optimal annual rainfall range for wheat is 25-150 cm (250-1500 mm).

claims.evidence_text: During the growing season specifically, wheat needs about 30-38 cm (12-15 inches) of water for a successful harvest.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat needs about 30-38 cm of water during the growing season for a successful harvest.

claims.evidence_text: A dry, warm ripening period with temperatures of 18°C or more is considered optimal for the final maturity stage.
claims.claim_summary: A dry, warm ripening period of 18 C or more is optimal for final maturity.

claims.evidence_text: The best soil for wheat is a fertile, deep, well-drained loam . According to Bayer Crop Science , wheat grows most successfully on loam and clay loam soils.
claims.claim_summary: Best soils for wheat are fertile, deep, well-drained loam and clay loam.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat requires a soil pH between 6.0 and 7.5 for optimal nutrient availability.
claims.claim_summary: Optimal soil pH for wheat is 6.0 to 7.5.

claims.evidence_text: According to the FAO, the broader optimum range extends from pH 6 to 8 .
claims.claim_summary: FAO broader optimum soil pH range for wheat is 6 to 8.

claims.evidence_text: Growing wheat in acidic soils below pH 5.5 leads to increased solubility of aluminium, manganese, and iron, which can become toxic and reduce plant growth.
claims.claim_summary: Below pH 5.5, aluminium/manganese/iron toxicity reduces wheat growth.

claims.evidence_text: Proper drainage is non-negotiable for wheat. Waterlogged soil suffocates roots, promotes fungal diseases, and leads to nutrient losses. Well-drained soils allow excess water to move away from the root zone
claims.claim_summary: Wheat requires well-drained soil; waterlogging suffocates roots and promotes disease.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat can tolerate a relatively high groundwater table – about 0.6 to 0.8 metres for sandy loam and silt loam soils, and 0.8 to 1 metre for clay soils – but prolonged shallow water tables significantly reduce yields.
claims.claim_summary: Tolerable groundwater table is 0.6-0.8 m (sandy/silt loam) and 0.8-1 m (clay soils).

claims.evidence_text: For good yields, the FAO recommends fertiliser inputs of up to 150 kg/ha nitrogen, 35-45 kg/ha phosphorus, and 25-50 kg/ha potassium.
claims.claim_summary: FAO recommends up to 150 kg/ha nitrogen for wheat.

claims.evidence_text: For good yields, the FAO recommends fertiliser inputs of up to 150 kg/ha nitrogen, 35-45 kg/ha phosphorus, and 25-50 kg/ha potassium.
claims.claim_summary: FAO recommends 35-45 kg/ha phosphorus for wheat.

claims.evidence_text: For good yields, the FAO recommends fertiliser inputs of up to 150 kg/ha nitrogen, 35-45 kg/ha phosphorus, and 25-50 kg/ha potassium.
claims.claim_summary: FAO recommends 25-50 kg/ha potassium for wheat.

claims.evidence_text: Rotating wheat with leguminous crops like chickpeas, lentils, or soybeans helps replenish soil nitrogen through biological fixation . Including non-cereal crops in the rotation also breaks pest and disease cycles
claims.claim_summary: Rotate wheat with legumes to replenish soil N and break pest/disease cycles.

claims.evidence_text: Reduced tillage or no-till practices help preserve soil aggregates and pore spaces, promoting better root growth and water movement.
claims.claim_summary: Reduced/no-till preserves soil aggregates and improves root growth and water movement.

claims.evidence_text: Leaving crop residues on the surface increases organic matter content and reduces erosion. However, in no-till systems, surface-applied nitrogen fertiliser can get tied up in crop residue and become temporarily unavailable
claims.claim_summary: Crop residues raise organic matter and cut erosion, but tie up surface-applied N in no-till.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat requires a deep soil... The best soil for wheat is a fertile, deep, well-drained loam.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat requires deep soil for rooting.
